paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 12: The Senior Sister (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a JC1 girl who is asked to step up as vice-captain of her school's netball team after the senior captain breaks her ankle, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
When Ms Karim called my name across the court that Tuesday afternoon, I assumed it was about my footwork. I jogged over and waited. 'Hui Min,' she said, eyes fixed on the empty space where Daphne usually warmed up, 'I want you to be vice-captain for the rest of the term.' I almost laughed. I was sure she had made a mistake.

Paragraph 2
Daphne had broken her ankle the previous Saturday during the semi-final. We had all heard the crack from the bench — a small dry sound, like a chopstick snapping. The ambulance had taken her to Singapore General Hospital, and the rest of us had ridden home in a bus so quiet that even the engine seemed embarrassed to be heard.

Paragraph 3
'Me?' I said to Ms Karim. 'But I — I don't talk in training.' She finally looked at me. 'Exactly,' she said. 'You watch. You wait. You see what others miss. That is also leading.' She walked off without giving me a chance to refuse. I stared at her back, dismissive of the suggestion. Vice-captain. Me. The girl who could not even raise her hand in GP class.

Paragraph 4
For three days I avoided thinking about it. I did, however, keep my Wednesday habit of visiting Daphne at her HDB flat in Toa Payoh, where she was stuck in a moon boot with three weeks of physiotherapy ahead. That afternoon I helped her down to the clinic at the void deck and waited outside while she did her exercises. We had been doing this every Wednesday since her surgery.

Paragraph 5
On the bus ride back, she finally asked. 'So,' she said, picking at a loose thread on her skirt, 'Ms Karim told me yesterday. You haven't said yes.' I looked out at the rain. 'I'm not a leader,' I said. 'I don't know what to say to them. I'm not — I'm not like you.' Daphne was quiet for a long moment.

Paragraph 6
'You think I knew what to say?' she said at last. 'Two years ago, when Captain Su Ling left for university, Ms Karim handed me the bib in the changing room and I locked myself in a toilet cubicle for fifteen minutes and cried.' I turned to look at her. Her ears had gone slightly red. 'I had not told anyone that before,' she added. 'Not even my mother.'

Paragraph 7
'But you're a good captain,' I said. The words came out smaller than I had intended. Daphne shook her head. 'Being scared is not what makes you not a captain, Hui Min. Being scared is part of the job. I was scared the whole first season. The girls only saw the part I let them see.' She looked at me properly. 'You are kind to them. You see them. That is the part I cannot teach.'

Paragraph 8
At training the next Tuesday, I stood at the side of the court. My heart was beating in my ears. Ms Karim raised an eyebrow, the way she did when she was offering you a door but not pushing you through it. I stepped forward. 'Right,' I said. My voice sounded thin and unfamiliar, but it carried. 'Passing drills. Pairs, three metres apart.' Twelve faces turned to me. I did not look at the empty space on the bench. Daphne, I knew, was there in another way.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. JC: Junior College; HDB: Housing & Development Board public flats; GP: General Paper, a core JC subject; bib: the marked vest worn by netball players.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, why did the narrator assume Ms Karim wanted to speak to her about her footwork? [1]
questions.answer: she could not imagine being called over for anything more important than a correction during training, so footwork was the most ordinary explanation she could think of

questions.stem: Q2(a). What does the simile 'a small dry sound, like a chopstick snapping' (Paragraph 2) achieve? [1]
questions.answer: it makes the breaking of Daphne's ankle feel disturbingly small and ordinary, like an everyday household sound, which makes the injury seem even more shocking to the team

questions.stem: Q2(b). What is the effect of describing the bus ride home as so quiet 'that even the engine seemed embarrassed to be heard' (Paragraph 2)? [1]
questions.answer: the personification of the engine being 'embarrassed' emphasises just how deeply silent and shaken the team was — the silence was unusual and heavy, not ordinary tiredness

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 3, how does Ms Karim turn the narrator's apparent weakness into a strength? [1]
questions.answer: the narrator sees her quietness as a reason she cannot lead, but Ms Karim reframes it as the ability to watch, wait and notice what others miss — calling that also a form of leading

questions.stem: Q4. Why is the detail that the narrator 'could not even raise her hand in GP class' (Paragraph 3) included? [1]
questions.answer: it shows how unconfident she is in front of others even in an academic setting, deepening the reader's sense of how unlikely a vice-captain she believes herself to be

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 4, what does the narrator's habit of visiting Daphne every Wednesday tell us about her? [1]
questions.answer: it shows that she is loyal, considerate and steady — she has been quietly caring for the injured captain over several weeks without being asked, which is itself a form of leadership

questions.stem: Q6(a). What does the word 'dismissive' (Paragraph 3) reveal about the narrator's first reaction to the offer of vice-captaincy? [1]
questions.answer: she rejected the idea in her head almost immediately, refusing to take it seriously / treating the suggestion as obviously wrong before even considering it

questions.stem: Q6(b). In Paragraph 6, why is it significant that Daphne admits she had 'not told anyone that before, not even my mother'? [1]
questions.answer: it shows how much Daphne trusts the narrator and how much she wants her to hear this — the confession is more powerful because Daphne has never shared it with even those closest to her

questions.stem: Q7. Explain in your own words what Daphne means in Paragraph 7 by 'Being scared is not what makes you not a captain, Hui Min. Being scared is part of the job.' [1]
questions.answer: she means that fear is something every captain feels, not a sign of being unfit for the role — feeling scared does not disqualify you from leading; learning to lead despite the fear is part of what leadership actually is

questions.stem: Q8. What does the description of Ms Karim's raised eyebrow as 'offering you a door but not pushing you through it' (Paragraph 8) suggest about her style as a coach? [1]
questions.answer: she invites her players to step up but lets them decide for themselves — she creates the opportunity without forcing anyone, treating them with respect

questions.stem: Q9. In Paragraph 8, why does the narrator describe her own voice as 'thin and unfamiliar, but it carried'? [1]
questions.answer: the voice is thin and unfamiliar because she has never spoken up at training before and is nervous, but the fact that it still 'carried' across the court shows she has begun to lead despite her fear

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of the final sentence 'Daphne, I knew, was there in another way' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: it shows that Daphne's example and her honest confession on the bus are now part of how Hui Min leads — even though Daphne is physically absent, her presence as a mentor and senior sister is with the narrator on the court

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling changes as she moves from the moment Ms Karim names her vice-captain to her first training session as a leader. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best summarises her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: dismissive / amused / proud / uncertain / understood / ready / suspicious / impatient. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Senior Sister' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift over the days — from being dismissive of the offer of vice-captaincy because she does not see herself as a leader, to being uncertain on the bus ride back from Daphne's physiotherapy as she tries to explain why she cannot accept, to feeling understood after Daphne confesses she once cried in a toilet cubicle the day she was made captain, ending ready at the next training session as she finally calls the first drill. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 as Ms Karim walks off after telling her she will be vice-captain and she stares at her back thinking 'Vice-captain. Of this team. Me'? [1]

Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 5 on the bus ride back from physiotherapy, looking out at the rain and telling Daphne 'I'm not a leader. I don't know what to say to them'? [1]

Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7, after Daphne tells her that being scared is part of the job and that she sees a kindness in Hui Min she herself cannot teach? [1]
Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 as she finally steps forward at training and calls the first drill, her voice thin but carrying across the court? [1]
